I'm trying to remember.
was having technical difficulties in trying to find your resume.

Ottawa.

A couple folks in Vancouver, Toronto, Calgary, probably missing some.
We're growing so fast at this point, honestly, and now that I'm on a different side of the business, sometimes I'm like, oh, my God, these new faces, what do you do?
It's a good problem to have.
It's exciting.
It's exciting.
It's
It's tough for Canadians, right?
It's still the economy here is very, very much reliant on oil, housing, banking, right?
So the startup culture is not necessarily something that thrives in Canada.
So when these opportunities come across, they're always worth exploring.
Interesting.
Yeah.
My last company I was at home base actually worked with a lot of Canadian folks, more Toronto side.
Yeah.
And the tech teams, they're interesting.
Like there is obviously tech, but it's different than I think what I'm used to.

And so like a lot of folks that know each other from like a different circle, like Bennett, like, well, Simple or Shopify or in, you know, kind of like if they've been at some of these places, they very likely know these other people.
It's very interesting.
I mean, the Bay has that as well.
Don't get me wrong.
It's actually, I'm constantly reminded how small the tech scene is.
It's funny here in Ottawa, was, Ottawa was like really the forefront of like essentially like optical networking back in the day.
So there's still like a huge tech park, but it's pretty much.
All R&D facilities now.
mean, Nortel was out of here.

I actually worked for the company who acquired them, and, like, they were responsible for laying all that initial, like, groundwork and infrastructure and developing kind of, like, all these optical networking protocols.

And it's funny now, there's still, like, thousands of tech workers here, but for the most part, they're all R&D facilities, like, big labs out of here, where usually we'll see HQs out of the States and then kind of leveraging, I guess what you could say is cheap Canadian engineering.
Interesting.
Interesting.
I wonder how that dynamic has changed.
I don't want to make this political, but, oh, man, with that dynamic, with those things between the two countries, it's so strange now.
Story for another day.
You're all good.
Well, great to connect, man.
I don't know if you got much background on me, but I can just do, like, a quick, you know, intro and just a little bit about my evolution here and give you a little context.
Perfect.
So I have kind of, I'm essentially leading on the sales side, wearing, likely will be wearing multiple hats, you know, in the future, helping out in different ways in the business.
I have done varying things here in my time.
I joined back in October.
I was leading the client services side of the business up until about May or so, maybe a little bit earlier.
And now leading on the sales side.
Previous to that, I actually was the first customer about a year ago.
And so part of the inspiration behind me stepping into this role was, you know, being the first customer, leading all the customer engagements, and just seeing that at both sides of it.
And also just being in the role of most people we're selling into, hopefully comes off as like a consultative, less pitchy, and that tends to like align fairly well with how today.
At least we handle our sales motion.
I won't go too in depth, but maybe we can later.
But the deals move very fast.
It's not unusual for us to close deals in two, maybe three calls.
There's certainly been times we've closed them in one.
But we tend to try to inspire a little bit, like what strategy could look like a bid.
And I think that's fine for now.
I will likely need to evolve that over time.
But, you know, given my background and experience, it kind of fits in with that kind of motion, right?
Previous to that, 19 years in both agencies, I did house Friday different stages.
A lot of my experience lies very in line with why people come to work with us.
I was brought on a lot of businesses that want to diversify their channels.
They don't want to be spending as much on paid search.
Usually they're spending way more in paid search than they would like.
spending time with
Their cacks are starting to inflate.
They need to diversify and find other channels that can be compatible for them.
And they need someone to help.
And that's usually why I get brought into these companies to help them figure that out.

And that tends to be sometimes we talk to folks that are like early and more kind of like they've heard or have experienced that journey other places or they're in the thick of that and they need help to help them get out of that.
And so based in Oakland, California, I grew up in New Hampshire.
So I'm from the East Coast initially, but I've been in the Bay for 13 years now.
So very much feels like home.
No plans to go back.
I do not miss winter and things like that.
It's part of what brought me out here, amongst other things, know, just career-wise, good place to be.
Anyways, yeah, I had a chance obviously to catch up on some of the past combos, but would love just, you know, quick go.
So, you know, I would like to just hear, like, and, you know, from your perspective, what led to the bit of, like, the evolution of career that led you to the interest in this role?
I'd love to just hear a bit about that.
Yeah, 100%.
So I actually kind of started my professional career doing public relations work.
So I was in-house working for one of those optical networking companies I mentioned earlier.
We're pretty closely with agencies and obviously we're close with sales team writing press releases and graphs.
I didn't go to school for communications, but essentially I'd been a sales intern for this company.
They offered me a 50K salary and pay the rest of my school off.
And I was, like, 20, 21.
was, like, yep, done deal.
So for a few years.
Better than I was earning at that age, if I remember correctly.
It was nice.
It obviously allowed me to get a jump start on saving and things, which has really helped.
But for a couple of years, I eventually got, like, I always enjoyed writing.
Like, I'd write thought leadership pieces.
Like,
So on behalf of our executives, like we do put together infographics, got to do fun things like design a newsroom over at Sienna.
But eventually it really turned into a lot of press releases and having legal go through press releases, rip them up.
It wasn't necessarily something I wanted to do anymore.
And plus I'd been working close to the sales team and I kind of figured how much they were all making.
So I was like, okay, I need to make a bit of a job switch.
So I actually started my sales career.
had the option of going the traditional SDR route or go sell cars because one of my parents' friends owned a dealership and I was like, chase the money.
So I actually went and sold cars.
I always joke I should have written a sitcom about that experience because it was just like absolutely wild.
And honestly, I'm not sure if you've seen it, but Shane Gillis' Tires on Netflix is crazy.
I watched like one or two episodes.
It wasn't that I was like not interested in it, but it was, you know, there's just so much TV nowadays.
Yeah, it's wild.
But that pretty much sums up that experience.
So anyways, COVID hit, and I was like, okay, it's a good time to transition, hold the shutdown, hit off.
So jumped into email marketing, did that for a little bit.
That was very much a sink or swim environment, really wasn't making any money there compared to what I was used to.
So had a recruiter knock on my door from a company called Agility.
They're based out of Ottawa, publication software.
So like media databases, aggregating media reports, things like that.
So traditionally earned media and was there and learned a lot there.
We had a good VP who actually taught me how to structure sales processes, run proper discoveries.
So I learned quite a bit there.
But I kept hearing about this thing like social listening over and over and over.
People kept asking for it.
So finally I was like, who's doing this?
Brandwatch became team out of Canada.
So jumped over to Brandwatch.
I've been here ever since for about three and a half years now.
And it's been really good.
It's paid for a wedding.
It's allowed me to grow my savings, have a little boy.
So, yeah, it's been a crazy year.
Got married and had a kid within like 11 months there.
Okay, cool, man.
I think the little bit of like crossover slash I interrupted.

yeah.

I've never bought one.

Yeah, so, I mean, like, the easiest way to describe it, like, from the top level is sort of punching something into a search engine, getting 60,000 links.
can take all that information, put it into visualizations, right?
So brands, like, are going to use it for everything from, like, content ideation, right, to want to identify audience demographics.

So it really does have quite a bit of use cases.

Metrics, like your general segment, let's say, or brand awareness.
can measure trends.
Brandwatch.
So if you really want to, okay, we're going to look at, say, the hard seltzer market.

Well, let's go figure out what that conversation looks like.
Who's talking about it?

Is there an opinion on it versus X?

Like it's all identifying those metrics is so brands can ideally start putting together some messaging.
Like when people are talking about purchasing a product, what are they talking about?
Is it value?
Is it price?
Is it ingredients?
Is it like kind of the calorie conversation, right?
Yep.
Kind of capped out their niche.
So it really allows you to drill pretty deep down into that data.
with Brandwatch, the nice thing is that Elon kind of changed the game with when he started charging for the API on Twitter to access that information.
So it's worked well for us just because the economy is a scale.
can afford to pay them.

What is the typical buying center?
So the other thing for Brandwatch, technically, we have, like, a social media management suite, too, something, like, similar to a Hootsuite or a Stroup.
So that's, typically, I don't work on those two too often anymore, but those are typically, it's, I'm kind of glad I'm out of that space, honestly, because it's turned into a race to the bottom.
That's typically what's called social leaders.
The consumer research.
Suite, which is kind of the good listening tool.
It's typically directors of marketing or communications and above.
That's like your intro, but typically we will kind of expand outwards from there.
Like you'll work your way into maybe an HR department because they want to monitor glass door reviews or understand what is driving like employees to go to one of their competitors, right?
You'll see, I did one.
was a big, basically only a ton of buildings in Manhattan.
And their security team wanted to know if the protests are around the building or if someone's climbed one of their skyscrapers and is posting Instagram photos.
Like they won't be aware of those conversations.
Like seeing sales teams use it, product teams use it.
Like if there's a gap, if customers are asking for something, but you typically start with the marketing communications and then expand outwards from there.
Got it.
And what's the like typical deal structure of that?
Is it like, are you doing a typical like SAS 12 month agreements or is it a more of a kind of like month to month?
Yeah.
everything is annualized contracts.
Typically we'll get, I'd say probably 30% of the deals are two or three year deals.
So that's pretty common with like government, higher ed, Boulder will do well with small deals.
So anyone kind of who's, I guess, more rigid in their processes, we'll see multi-years come on.
But annual agreements, we'll typically see like that product starts at roughly 30k annual or run up pretty quickly into like the millions of dollars.
But the average deal size for what I work with is, it counts up to usually 5,000 employees.
Typically you see it around 50, 60k per year.
Per year.
Yeah.
Got it.
And something like that.
I mean, how are you all making sure people are leveraging it appropriately and like, you know, helping inspire that?
Like, are you finding a lot of prospects you talk to come to you with a very specific set of use cases and wanting you to validate, can you do this or not?
Or how often are you having to kind of like connect the dots for them and educate them of like, this is what this unlocks for you?
Yeah, I mean, it depends.
So on the inbounds, like obviously there's a level of education there.
It's pretty strong.
Or the outbound stuff, self-booking, I'm still typically going after someone who is familiar with the term like social listening or knows what bullying is or what a query structure is, right?
Like, they're not even at that level.
Typically, no organization is going to invest 30, 50 grand into a tool like that.
So there has to be some familiarity.
But like another thing we've done pretty well is kind of like steal business from even our parent company, Scission, or like Meltwater, those traditional media monitoring tools, right?
So like, if I can prove your audience is having conversations about you on Reddit, why the hell do you care about these syndications from your press releases?
You're paying $1,800 a piece of , like.
So that's been a conversation that's kind of expanded opportunities for sure, but I wouldn't say there's a ton of educating going on.

It might be a few things you're pointing out, and typically what we'll do too is a lot of proof of concepts for those folks who are still kind of like, I'm not 100% sure there's enough conversation out there.
Now, most of the time, I'll kind of build a relatively simple dashboard that's going to cover off two or three use cases for them.
If we're going to be a bigger player, multiple stakeholders involved, we do have solutions consultants I can always leverage.
Okay, cool, cool.
Just to kind of like, this is super helpful just to give me a little bit more context, just to like kind of dig in a little bit deeper and appreciate the typical motion.

Can you like walk me through like a recent deal, ideally like where you sold to a marketing leader and would love just like, you know, context of how they came to you, what they're solving for, et cetera.
If you could just walk me like beginning to end to that.
Yeah, 100%.
So I'll use the example, actually.
It's kind of a fun one.
You may have seen the documentary.
It's called The Durst Organization.
I'm not sure if you've ever seen The Jinx.
was on HBO.
The Jinx?
Yeah.
Yeah, it's kind a crazy story.
So essentially, this is a family out of Manhattan.
They own a good piece of One World Trade Center.
They own blocks of Manhattan, old, old monitoring.
Yeah, yeah.
Essentially, there was one brother who was like a serial killer, like literally, and the guy had gotten away with all this stuff for years of his life.
His name was Robert Durst, and then his brother, because Robert was obviously a little crazy when the dad passed away, Robert was the older one.
He wasn't given the business his brother was.
So this all came up.
Anyways, the documentary.
This is right up my wife's alley, first of all.
We'll spoil it for you, but how this kind of ends up in Kyle is because a movie was made about him for Ryan Gosling.
He was such an egotistical, crazy guy.
That movie's wrong.
That's wrong.
I want to talk to the basically the director of the movie.
And the director's like a normal.
It's like Hollywood filmmakers, like I got a documentary, this guy's crazy.

So how it all wraps up, I won't ruin it for you as well, but essentially this family wanted to monitor mentions of their brand and they didn't care about all the conversation from Robert Durst, the direct organization, things like this, right?
So I had initially come in and I was speaking with this guy, he's actually on the communications team and they hadn't been using anything.
They had used competitors in the past, but their big issue was like all these tools, they just pull in a bunch of noise.
So it was just a matter of getting the data infrastructure set up correctly to get things set up.
So that was one, it probably took six to nine months.
mean, the decision had been made relatively early on in that sales process, they had, of course, a crazy legal team, which dragged on for about three months.
But that one was interesting because I initially started with the communications team, expanded into their marketing departments, their security also came to play.
So there's kind of three departments really.
Yeah, that one.
It was something where they're trying to play some hardball, but lucky enough, Bandwatch, we're hosting these masterclasses we do in New York City.
So after probably like three to five Zoom calls, had done a little bit of a proof of concept, had obviously had some side conversations.
Like my main champion, this guy yesterday, was fantastic.
I was able to start texting him relatively early on in the process.
So knowing when they would go back and have these internal meetings after, I could be like, hey, how did it go?
Like what, what came up?
What, what should I prepare for?
Kind of follow up when there's multiple stakeholders with like kind of individual emails after like, hey, I heard you ask this question.
Here's some documentation on it.
So I'd, I'd done that and we ended up inviting the team to, to one of these masterclasses at Bandwatch hosts every once in a while.
Met them in person, again, kind of introduced them to, to the team they would be working with post-sale.
That's really what ended up selling them.
Went through the legal, we ended up closing off that deal.
And that was a good one, probably took care of, with 70% of my quota.
Um,
For a single quarter.
So definitely it was a needle mover, but it was a lot of coordinating.
If I was to summarize that one, coordinating internally, making sure like a little thing I'll do when I work with others, like joining a call is all like a dummy thing on the calendar.
Solutions consultant jumping in or my VP or someone else is coming to the call.
They'll see beside their calendar.
Okay.
It's essentially a brief.
Here's who's coming from the customer side or the prospect side.
Here's their main pain points.
Here's their titles.
Here's what they care about.
Here's what we know.
Here's the current state.
Here's the ideal state.
Yeah.
Summarize that.
So they can jump on five minutes before the call, review it and know what they're talking about.
And then obviously coordinating on their side, Like getting a good champion.
Hey, how did things go?
Like who's a possible detractor here?
Like who owns the current process that you're trying to change, right?
So fine.
It's typically do good discovery up front.
Find a champion who's willing to work with you.
think that's a big one.
Someone's not going to give you the time of day.
So it's typically...
Time to move on, right?
They're not taking it seriously, so identify your buyers and just stay organized from there and be professional.
Cool.
Love it.
How much background have you come up?

Like, I assume, obviously, you've had a couple conversations, so I'm assuming this shouldn't be too out of your wheelhouse, but I'm curious if you're kind of trying to think about the typical priorities versus the marketers that you sell today versus the marketer.
I mean, not that they're different people, but how do you view potentially the priorities of those that are coming to growth?
What are those top three priorities for them that we need to be mindful of?
And where is there overlap between kind of what you had on the brand watch side?
I'm curious to get your perspective on where you feel strong and where you feel like you need some coaching and some reps into the new role.
Yeah, so before I dive into that, this is something I was curious about.
I spoke to Bridget a little bit.
So like today, growthx is essentially acting as an agency, right?
You're helping with content marketing.
So are there certain forms of content marketing you were focusing on more so than others?
Is it blogs, infographics, landing pages?
Like, are you doing social?
Like, obviously, you're trying to reduce people's CAC and reduce paid spend, go the organic route.
So what is the actual deliverables look like to the end client here that are really helping you win?
Yeah, it's a combination of different things, right?
Like, I think one of the challenges we have at times is, like, there is a broadness of use cases that we could theoretically help folks out with and that we've done.
And at times, we talk to prospects that have, you know, clearly aren't sure where we fit into the spectrum of content.
So there is, like, a question, right?
But largely, there's, like, two core areas that are content.
starting point.
Focuses on typically, at least to start, and then it can broaden, and we can do different things.
Editorial focus content, some longer form content that could be blogged, but not necessarily limited to blogs.
The reason I say that is sometimes you can have longer form content.
You're building some sort of new, useful experience that sits in the non-blog section of your website.
And if you can find those hooks for certain customers, that can be quite fruitful.
And then there is programmatic content, and so that is like highly templatized content, where you usually are going after some set of variants, right?
Like, for example, Vappy is one of our customers.
We just spun up a bunch of programmatic pages for them, where they're a voice AI agent, and basically there's all these different use cases you could apply with Vappy to.
And so we actually did this fun programmatic play, where we literally are creating these prompts for their product.
lot.
lot of Yeah.
That's Really?
it's I think, we're
Literally, can interact with them, and there's one for cancellations and rescheduling and just all these different use cases that we identified.
And so we're creating all of those different pages, and it follows a consistent template, right?
You figure out that strategy, you figure out the variants, and you go after it.
So that's two of the basic flavors that we'll operate within, but certainly not limited to that.
You know, that's just where it's typically a good starting place.
But for Udemy right now, we're talking with them and just about to close them to build a newsletter for them.
And that's going to have both an email component to it, obviously, but also a web experience that powers that content and is more comprehensive.

And so at the end of the day, like, really, we want our customers to operate more like publishers and help them get to that high volume scale of, like, developing really amazing content for their audience.
And so...
so...
Analyze, you know, can certainly, SEO can be a big focus, but I think the key here is we do not want to be known as just an SEO agency.
That is pretty limiting for us.
If we get compared, and we certainly do, to SEO agencies, people aren't going to be thinking about us in the right way in terms of our value.
Okay.
Yeah, so, like, interestingly enough, I think what the overlap is, is, today, a lot of times what happens is we sell the dream.
People, everyone wants to make data-driven decisions, right?
It's like, oh, yeah, I don't want to do this now.
It's, so people will come in, they'll see our proof of concept, they will love it.
They have the data at their hands.
The issue is they typically have a gap where they don't have the bandwidth, the resources, the drive to actually take that data and convert it into probably net.
Closer to an end, deliverable with what you guys are currently delivering right now.
So, I think the use case is similar, and you're solving the problem that sometimes are a problem.
Our tool has where we've got a decent amount of churn.
It's always seems to be kind of, it's not even user adoption.
It's the actual options.
Like we'll give you the information to put together something useful, but you still need to go and create the content yourself.
Right.
And that gap that a ton of organizations have.
And I think that's probably the gap where you guys are coming to fill in, which is a pain point I see literally every day that a customer organization.
It's a common thing.
Yeah.
People buy software.
They're inspired to, you know, rigid and for, you know, real pain points for sure.
But then they get pulled into a variety of different directions.
may be underestimating the complexity of like where that fits into their current plans.
Obviously that doesn't always happen, but I've been on plenty of teams that have bought software.
That's just like, I guess we didn't really need this.
Like you're barely using it.
So yeah, it's yeah.
We find there's like a lot of, it's.

Actually, just where things are going and, you know, in related to things associated with AI and all that lines of strategy is like, yeah, there's a lot of teams that want to adopt it, but also they, and do, but they also just like, aren't going to have the capacity to build some of the things we've built and need help across like, how do they think about also their strategy, like not just build the things that the components to develop the workflows that we do, but also like, how do you adapt your strategy?
based off of that and how to think about that.
you know, so yeah, that's been a bit of our, I think what's helped us, helped us cut through the noise of like, you know, there's like different extremes, right?
There's certainly we're not, we're, and we're going to be less and less alone in this sort of like play in this space.
But, you know, those that are just like pure software and those are just pure services.
And it's like this, you know, middle point that a lot of people need some help.
How do you.
How do guys scope out your projects?
When I was talking to Bridget, she said right now, it seems like you're getting a lot of people in maybe two, three months, maybe 15K a month, kind of starting them out.
Some people are sticking around, others aren't.
Are you pricing based off of like billable hours?
Is it the actual content you're promising going out?
How are, I guess, the clients determining whether it was a good investment or not?
Yeah, we are doing, we've tweaked this a little bit recently, what Bridget was referring to there, is we start with what we call strategy sprints now and the intention of this.
Essentially, how we used to do it before was we used to sell 12-month deals with 90-day pilots, essentially, and you could opt out at the 90-day mark.
And what was effective about that is it de-risks the deal a bit, and so it made it a lot easier to close the deals.
But we started getting...
Too many folks that were opting out of pilots and that, like, honestly, just, like, we weren't doing enough expectation setting or we weren't setting our internal team up for success.
Like, just a lot of, like, natural growing pain type things of the company that was growing fast.
And so we have a ton of learnings from that.
And so the intention of the strategy sprint phase is, like, we're trying to figure out if it's a fit for us.

We're either trying to do the same or building a lot, laying a lot of critical foundation in terms of strategy and building the roadmap and defining what, like, success looks like and trying to de-risk a long-term engagement where we feel super confident and we have context of what that long-term engagement would be.
And it also provides us the ability to more appropriately scope what that engagement would be.
We don't want to get caught up in overselling or underselling.
If we can find that sweet spot, that's when they should be a bit stickier.
And we've been on both sides of that spectrum for sure at times where like we oversell an account, you know, we've had some deals 40 plus K a month.
And that comes with a lot of expectations.
And some of those, we still have those today and they're going pretty good.
But, you know, in that, like once you pass 20 K per month, it's just that bar is just super, super high.

And it's not that we don't have confidence in us, you know, like when the right situations hitting that bar, it's just more at times it might not even be realistic for us to like we can do everything in our power and feel good about our outputs.
But still, you know, the way you're going to be thought about at that level is going to be different.
And so today how we treat it is like relatively about like a flat rate of about 15 K per month to start into the.
The execution phase, the idea is that it's defined through the strategy sprint phase.
It's gotten a bit more murky in terms of how we actually price to that number than it used to be.
Some of that is somewhat intentional, more experimenting to see how far we can take that.
It's meant to give us flexibility, essentially.
But it used to be very much based off of words per month and then varying ranges based off of words per month.
Not that there was many.

## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview with Rob Nault for a Founding Account Executive role at growthx.ai](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Rob has a diverse background in PR, car sales, and SaaS sales, currently at Brandwatch selling social listening tools](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=375.0)
  - [growthx.ai offers content marketing services with a focus on editorial and programmatic content, aiming to help companies reduce CAC and diversify from paid search](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=1350.0)
  - [The company is in a growth phase, exploring potential software offerings and refining its sales process for its current agency-style model](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=2336.0)

## Topics

### Rob's Background and Career Progression

  - [Started in PR for an optical networking company](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=375.0)
  - [Transitioned to car sales for higher earning potential](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=444.0)
  - [Moved into email marketing, then to Agility (publication software)](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=487.0)
  - [Currently at Brandwatch for 3.5 years selling social listening tools](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=530.0)
  - [Average deal size at Brandwatch: $50-60k annually for companies up to 5,000 employees](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=873.0)

### growthx.ai's Current Offering and Business Model

  - [Primarily offering content marketing services (editorial and programmatic content)](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=1350.0)
  - [Typical engagement: $15k/month, starting with a strategy sprint phase](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=1707.0)
  - [Focus on reducing customer acquisition costs (CAC) and diversifying from paid search](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=1520.0)
  - [Challenges in measuring and reporting ROI, especially in early stages of engagement](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=1577.0)
  - [Exploring potential software offerings and platform development](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=2802.0)

### Sales Process and Challenges

  - [No SDR/BDR team, all inbound leads currently](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=2510.0)
  - [Two-call process: intro call (30 min) for qualification and initial excitement, second call for deeper discovery and potential close](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3250.0)
  - [Difficulty in effectively demonstrating value in early calls due to the nature of the service](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3250.0)
  - [Experimenting with pricing and packaging to find the right fit for customers](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3693.0)

### Future Direction and Potential Software Development

  - [Developing internal platform for workflow management and content creation](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3224.0)
  - [Exploring potential standalone apps (e.g., advanced fact-checker)](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3224.0)
  - [Considering how to monetize platform access for customers in the future](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3224.0)
  - [Balancing managed services with potential self-serve options](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3224.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Kyle to discuss with Bridget about moving Rob to the presentation round](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3548.0)
  - [Rob to review Marcel's workshop recap (posted \~2 months ago) for additional context on growthx.ai's approach](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3637.0)
  - [Rob to keep an eye on the website for any additional resources to learn more about the company's mindset and offerings](https://fathom.video/share/AngigsTxR2HU6MuU6sxELxx53ydxyzkn?tab=summary&timestamp=3623.0)
